content:
# Philippians Chapter 4 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 196-197 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHP_004_page_001.jpg, PHP_004_page_002.jpg

---

**1** So then, my brothers and sisters — you whom I love and long for, my joy and my crown — stand firm in the Lord, my dear ones. This is how you must stand.

**2** I urge Euodia and I urge Syntyche to come to agreement in the Lord.

**3** Yes, and I ask you too, my true partner, help these women. They fought side by side with me for the gospel, along with Clement and my other co-workers, whose names are written in the book of life.

**4** Rejoice in the Lord always! I will say it again: Rejoice!

**5** Let your gentleness be evident to everyone. The Lord is near.

**6** Do not be anxious about anything. Instead, in every situation, bring your requests to God through prayer and petition, with thanksgiving.

**7** And the peace of God, which surpasses all human understanding, will stand guard over your hearts and minds in Christ Jesus.

**8** Finally, brothers and sisters, fill your minds with whatever is true, whatever is worthy of respect, whatever is right, whatever is pure, whatever is lovely, whatever is admirable — if anything is excellent or praiseworthy, dwell on these things.

**9** Put into practice everything you learned and received from me, everything you heard me say and saw me do. And the God of peace will be with you.

**10** I rejoice greatly in the Lord that at last you have renewed your concern for me. You were always concerned, of course — you simply had no opportunity to show it.

**11** I am not saying this because I am in need. For I have learned to be content in whatever circumstances I find myself.

**12** I know what it is to have nothing, and I know what it is to have plenty. I have been initiated into the secret of living in every possible situation — whether well-fed or hungry, whether in abundance or in want.

**13** I can face anything through the one who gives me strength.

**14** Still, it was good of you to share in my troubles.

**15** And you Philippians know that in the early days of the gospel, when I left Macedonia, no church entered into a partnership of giving and receiving with me — no one except you alone.

**16** For even while I was in Thessalonica, you sent me aid more than once when I was in need.

**17** Not that I am looking for a gift; what I am looking for is the profit that accumulates to your account.

**18** I have been paid in full and have more than enough. I am fully supplied now that I have received from Epaphroditus what you sent — a fragrant offering, a sacrifice acceptable and pleasing to God.

**19** And my God will fully supply every need of yours according to his glorious riches in Christ Jesus.

**20** To our God and Father be glory forever and ever. Amen.

**21** Greet every one of God's people in Christ Jesus. The brothers and sisters who are with me send their greetings.

**22** All of God's people here send you greetings, especially those who belong to Caesar's household.

**23** The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Key Dynamic Choices

- **v. 1 — "my joy and my crown"** (Greek: *chara kai stephanos*): The Sinaiticus reads ΧΑΡΑ ΚΑΙ ΣΤΕΦΑΝΟΣ. The *stephanos* is not a royal diadem but a victory wreath — the laurel given to athletes and conquerors. Paul sees the Philippians themselves as both his deep delight and his victory prize. I kept the paired nouns bare and forceful rather than expanding the metaphor, because the directness is the point: *you are my joy, you are my crown.*

- **v. 3 — "my true partner"** (Greek: *gnesie syzuge*): The Sinaiticus reads ΓΝΗΣΙΕ ΣΥΖΥΓΕ. The word *syzygos* literally means "yoke-fellow" — someone harnessed alongside you in shared labor. The identity of this person is unknown. Some have speculated it is a proper name ("Syzygus"), but the adjective *gnesie* ("genuine, true") makes better sense as a description: "you who are truly yoked with me." I rendered it "true partner" to convey the depth of co-laboring.

- **v. 5 — "The Lord is near"** (Greek: *ho kyrios engys*): This phrase carries deliberate ambiguity — it could mean the Lord is near in presence (spatial/spiritual proximity) or near in time (the return is imminent). I preserved the brevity and left both meanings open, as the Greek does.

- **v. 7 — "will stand guard over"** (Greek: *phrouresei*): The Sinaiticus reads ΦΡΟΥΡΗΣΕΙ. This is a military term — to garrison, to post a sentinel. Paul pictures God's peace not as a passive feeling but as an armed guard posted at the gate of the heart and mind. "Stand guard over" captures this active, protective force more vividly than the traditional "keep" or "guard."

- **v. 12 — "I have been initiated into the secret"** (Greek: *memyemai*): The Sinaiticus reads ΜΕΜΥΗΜΑΙ. This verb comes from the mystery religions — to be initiated into hidden knowledge, to learn the secret rites. Paul borrows the language of pagan initiation to describe his own spiritual formation: he has been let in on the deep secret of contentment in all circumstances. "Initiated into the secret" preserves this striking resonance.

- **v. 13 — "I can face anything through the one who gives me strength"**: The Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΝΤΑ ΙΣΧΥΩ ΕΝ ΤΩ ΕΝΔΥΝΑΜΟΥΝΤΙ ΜΕ. The Greek says literally "I have strength for all things in the one empowering me." Note that Sinaiticus does not include the later addition "Christ" (Χριστω) after "the one who strengthens me" — it simply reads "the one empowering me." I rendered *panta ischyo* as "I can face anything" rather than the common "I can do all things," because the context is endurance through hardship and plenty, not unlimited capability. Paul is speaking of resilience, not omnipotence.

- **v. 18 — "a fragrant offering"** (Greek: *osmen euodias*): The Sinaiticus reads ΟΣΜΗΝ ΕΥΩΔΙΑΣ. This is drawn directly from Old Testament sacrificial language — the "aroma of a sweet smell" that rises to God from an acceptable sacrifice (cf. Genesis 8:21, Exodus 29:18). Paul elevates the Philippians' material gift into the realm of sacred worship. Their money becomes incense.

- **v. 22 — "Caesar's household"** (Greek: *Kaisaros oikias*): This does not mean the emperor's family but rather the vast network of imperial slaves, freedmen, and bureaucrats attached to the Roman administration. That believers existed within this structure — the very machinery of empire — is a quietly astonishing detail. I kept the phrase direct and let its implications speak.

### Manuscript Observations
The Sinaiticus text of Philippians 4 spans two pages. On page 196, the chapter begins near the bottom of the fourth column, following Philippians 3:18ff in columns 3-4. On page 197, the chapter continues across the first three columns: column 1 picks up around v. 5, column 2 around v. 13, and column 3 around v. 21, with the chapter's conclusion and the subscript ΠΡΟΣ ΦΙΛΙΠΠΗΣΙΟΥΣ visible before Colossians begins in column 4. The text is written in the characteristic Sinaiticus uncial hand with scriptio continua. The text aligns closely with the critical text, with the notable absence of "Christ" in v. 13 as discussed above.

— PNEUMA
